data.title: Trump says Russia, China not 'participating' in Iran war 'in my opinion'
data.description:
President Trump on Friday said he takes at face value testimony from the leaders of China and Russia that they are not providing weapons to Iran or otherwise “participating” in its war with the United States and Israel.

The president’s defense, issued in a statement on his social media site Truth Social, comes after Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth told lawmakers that Russia and China are “enabling Iran.” 

“President Xi, at our recent meeting in Beijing, China, told me that he would not, under any circumstances, give or sell Weapons to the Islamic Republic of Iran — And that statement included Chinese Companies,” Trump wrote, referring to Chinese President Xi Jinping. 

“Considering our relationship, I take him at his word and, besides, l am doing him very big favors, also. Likewise, President Putin … told me that he would not sell Weapons to Iran.”

Trump continued, “Therefore, two major Countries that people speak of often in terms of Iran are, in my opinion, not participating. If they did, it would be very bad for them — Certainly not in their best interests.”

Hegseth told lawmakers on Tuesday that Russia and China, “at different levels,” are “enabling what Iran is doing.” 

Gen. Dan Caine, the chair of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said such information is classified. 

“I’d rather not opine on specifics because that would divulge what we know, and I never want the adversary to know what I know, I just want to know it,” he said.

In March, CNN reported that unnamed U.S. officials said Russia is providing Iran with Russian satellite imagery to track the location and movement of American troops.  

Reuters reported this week that Iranian strikes on CIA facilities in the Gulf have prompted U.S. intelligence analysts to investigate whether Russia has provided targeting information or advanced drone technology to Iran. 

Iran may also have used Chinese missiles and long-range early-warning radar during the war, sources told NBC News in May. Experts also say Chinese satellites have provided Iran with enhanced targeting data.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who also serves as national security adviser, pushed back on reporters asking if he had raised during a meeting with Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov the issue of Russia sharing targeting intelligence. 

“Who says they are?” he asked twice and suggested Hegseth was not specific in his accusations. 

“Let’s imagine that were true. I certainly wouldn’t discuss it in the press,” he continued. 

“Nothing that Iran is doing — nothing that anyone’s doing to help Iran is in any way increasing their ability to target Americans.”

data.title: Michigan Democrat refuses to say if she'd back El-Sayed if he wins primary
data.description:
A Michigan Democrat is drawing attention with her refusal to back Democratic Senate hopeful Abdul El-Sayed over his campaigning with political commentator Hasan Piker and disgraced Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner.

Rep. Hillary Scholten (D-Mich.), in an interview Wednesday with CNN’s Jake Tapper, criticized El-Sayed, who is running for retiring Sen. Gary Peters’s (D-Mich.) seat against Rep. Haley Stevens (D-Mich.). Scholten endorsed Stevens in the race in April 2025.

Scholten pointed to comments made by Piker about 9/11.

She said El-Sayed chose “to campaign with Hasan Piker, who said America deserved September 11th. Sure, he went on to disavow those comments, but I’m telling you, nobody on my team would ever, you know, come even close to making a comment like that.”

Piker, a left-wing Twitch streamer, apologized for these comments in 2019, calling them “inappropriate.”

“I should’ve used more precise and better use of the language there,” Piker said during an interview with fellow political commentator Cenk Uygur.

Scholten said she would give Piker “credit for disavowing it after the fact. But the reality is, he [El-Sayed] also didn’t have to choose to campaign with Hasan Piker.”

El-Sayed had declined opportunities to disavow Piker’s views, referring to attempts to do so as a “gotcha game.” He has also defended his appearance with Piker at Michigan State University while on the campaign trail.

“Abdul El-Sayed and Graham Platner campaigned together,” Scholten added. 

El-Sayed and Platner never campaigned together but did “split send two fundraising emails,” David Weigel from Semafor reported on the social media platform X.

Platner won the Maine Senate Democratic primary and was set to take on Sen. Susan Collins’s (R-Maine). He dropped his bid when allegations from past partners surfaced, accusing him of sexual misconduct during their relationships. 

Scholten remained critical of these associations and their effect on the Democratic Party.

“I think that condoning behavior like this from candidates like Abdul El-Sayed is taking us in a direction that we don’t want to go as a party. I don’t need $20 million to study why young men stopped voting for the Democratic Party. Look at the message that we’re sending to our veterans if we’re willing to embrace or even brush aside comments like that. I would never stand on a stage or be at a rally with someone like Hasan Piker because he chose to say comments like that,” she said.

data.title: Pennsylvania governor declares LeBron James Day as Sixers set to sign NBA star
data.description:
LeBron James is reportedly set to finish his storied career as a Philadelphia 76er, and Pennsylvania’s governor celebrated the news with an official proclamation.

“By the power vested in the me as the Governor of the great Commonwealth of Pennsylvania and a die-hard Sixers fan… I hereby proclaim today to be LEBRON JAMES DAY,” Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) wrote in a social media post on Friday.

The proclamation laid out James’ accomplishments in the league, including his four NBA titles and 22 times appearing the league’s All Star Game. James began his career in Cleveland, before winning titles with the Miami Heat and Los Angeles Lakers.

Shapiro’s post came just hours after ESPN reported James was set to sign a two-year, $8 million deal with the 76ers beginning this season.

James, 41, in a string of posts on X talked about his most recent stint in free agency, saying “the last few weeks have really been special.”

“I’ve never been able to have no clue what to do and take real time to just think. I’ve had an incredible few months with all the people I love trying to figure it all out,” James wrote. “This is my last decision. I’m not going for money. I’m not going for family. What am I really playing for at this point? I still want to sacrifice. I still want to work. I still want to grind. I still want to compete, to win and to have a chance at the feeling of winning another championship.”

James will join Philadelphia stars Joel Embiid and Tyrese Maxey in the city of “Brotherly Love,” which has not won an NBA title since 1983.

data.title: Massie: Hegseth is breaking law with Iran war
data.description:
Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) accused Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth of “breaking the law” on Thursday, arguing the Trump administration is attempting to portray the Iran conflict as two separate wars in order to avoid seeking congressional authorization.

Massie made the claim in response to reporting from The New York Times that said the Pentagon lowered the number of U.S. service members listed as killed in the war from 18 to 14.

“Let me explain this absurd ruse: The Pentagon is pretending there have been two Iran wars separated by a brief cease-fire,” he wrote on social platform X. “The reality: By going more than 90 days without congressional authorization.”

The Kentucky Republican, who lost his reelection bid in May after President Trump backed his opponent in the Kentucky GOP primary, added that Hegseth “IS BREAKING THE LAW and must be held accountable.”

According to the Times, three military officials said the Trump administration removed the names of four service members killed last weekend — three in Jordan and one in northern Iraq — from its casualty count because their deaths occurred after a ceasefire was declared. Trump earlier this week attended the dignified transfer of the same troops.

Acting Pentagon press secretary Joel Valdez told the outlet that the lower count was due to “temporary data disruptions” and “site anomalies” that he said were being resolved.

“The department attempted to explain the anomalies to The New York Times ahead of publication, but given their lack of knowledge in this area, they proceeded to write a non-story,” he said. “We remain committed to ensuring the utmost accuracy of all casualty reporting.”

Massie argued that treating the renewed fighting as a separate conflict would allow the administration to restart the timeline established under the War Powers Resolution of 1973. Fighting in the region began in late February, but a temporary ceasefire was reached during now-stalled peace talks.

The 14-point memorandum of understanding signed by the U.S. and Iran last month, which included another pause in fighting, has since crumbled as both accused the other of violating the terms. The U.S. military completed its 13th night of strikes early Friday.

Under the War Powers Act, a president must end unauthorized U.S. military involvement within 60 days unless Congress formally declares war or otherwise approves the operation. The president may also receive an additional 30 days to safely withdraw U.S. troops.

The House earlier this week passed a war powers resolution that would limit the military operation in the Middle East. Lawmakers in the Senate, however, failed to pass a similar measure.

data.title: Manchin launches effort to elect independent candidates to Congress
data.description:
Former Sen. Joe Manchin (I-W.Va.) is returning to politics by announcing a new organization aimed at recruiting and electing independent candidates to Congress.

Manchin, 78, launched the Independent Leadership Council (ILC), which is focused on building support and infrastructure for candidates running outside the Democratic and Republican parties in congressional races. He’ll lead the group alongside his daughter, Heather Manchin, a former pharmaceutical executive.

“We’re not trying to start another party,” Manchin said in an interview with CNN. “We’re trying to get a person who can be committed to – and totally committed to – being an independent with an independent voice. They can vote with Democrats and Republicans when they’re right and speak out against them when they’re wrong.”

Manchin, who left the Democratic Party before retiring from the Senate in 2025 after decades as a Democrat, told CNN he has no plans to run for office again, but wants to help elect candidates willing to work across party lines.

“I don’t see me getting back into the fray, I really don’t,” he said. “Now I’m trying to help people around the country that want that same outcome.”

The former West Virginia senator convened prospective candidates and political strategists in West Virginia this week for the group’s inaugural summit.

Attendees included Rep. Kevin Kiley (I-Calif.), the Republican-turned-Independent who faces a contentious reelection bid this year, independent Montana Senate candidate Seth Bodnar and Iowa House candidate Michael Brigford.

The event also highlighted ILC’s target races in priority states such as Alaska, California, Iowa, Montana and Washington.

“The gathering served as both a strategy session and a call to action for Americans who believe the country’s most important problems cannot be solved through partisan warfare alone,” ILC said in a statement.

If the council decides to back candidates, the Manchins said they could campaign alongside them by helping raise money and organize in their districts. Heather Manchin described the effort as a test case that could expand if successful.

“Our hope is that this is kind of our pilot experiment, trying to prove it can be done,” she told CNN.

The initiative comes as 45 percent of U.S. adults now identify as political independents, according to a Gallup poll. 

Independents have made up the largest percentage of voters for more than a decade, as Americans grow more disenchanted with the current two-party system and even as Republicans and Democrats continue to dominate elected offices.

data.title: Immigration is a growth strategy for the states that need it most
data.description:
After another Independence Day of parades, flags and speeches about the American experiment, the harder question is whether the country still believes in one of the forces that helped build it: people coming here to build a better life.

That story is not only about America’s past. It is also about the country’s economic future.

Unfortunately, a recent wave of immigration restrictions, including fewer green cards awarded, travel bans from various countries, reduced refugee and asylum grants and others, has resulted in a drop in immigration. According to the U.S. Census Bureau, net international migration peaked at 2.7 million in 2024, fell to 1.3 million in 2025 and is projected to drop to 321,000 in 2026, which the bureau calls a historic decline.

Restrictions on immigration are based on several concerns. But a major justification is a perceived negative economic effect of immigration, particularly on state and local budgets.

Those concerns should not simply be dismissed. Any growing population creates demands on public services. But a recent study I coauthored suggests the claim that immigrants are simply a drain on state economies does not fit the evidence.

Using data for every U.S. state between 2008 and 2023, we find that a 1 percent increase in a state’s population from immigration is associated with a 1.5 percent increase in private sector GDP for the value of goods and services produced in the average state. That’s a larger percentage increase in income than in population.

The effect also varies dramatically by state. In our simulations, a 1 percent increase in a state’s total population coming entirely from immigrants raises private-sector GDP by anywhere from 0.57 percent to 4.41 percent, depending on the state. 

States with small immigrant populations, such as West Virginia (2.97 percent), Montana (4.04 percent), Mississippi (4.02 percent), North Dakota (2.48 percent) and Wyoming (2.89 percent), show large effects. Similarly, states with high levels of economic freedom, such as New Hampshire (3.81 percent), South Dakota (3.14 percent), Idaho (2.7 percent) and Tennessee (3.27 percent), show large impacts.

These results have important implications for our nation, with recent demographic trends raising concerns about the future of the U.S. economy.

According to the Congressional Budget Office (CBO), the rate of U.S. population growth is expected to slow over the next 30 years, from an average of 0.3 percent per year for the next 10 years to 0.1 percent per year from 2037 to 2056. Moreover, as fertility rates decline, net migration will become increasingly important, with the U.S. population projected to decline starting in 2030 without immigration.

An aging U.S. population accentuates the concern. CBO projects that the ratio of people between 25 and 64 to those over 65 will decline from 2.7 now to 2.2 over the next three decades. This means a lower working-age population, fewer people who typically innovate, fewer contributions to Social Security and other programs and fewer people to care for aging adults.

There are also important implications for states. Although immigration law is federal, national legislators can reform immigration law for the benefit of their state. In particular, representatives from rural states and those with less burdensome government (those that are traditionally red) would benefit the most from immigration reform.  

That is the irony at the center of today’s debate: Many of the states most worried about immigration are also those worried about population loss and workforce shortages. They have the strongest economic case for immigration.

Moreover, states can enact policies that enable them to benefit more from immigration.  States can lower tax burdens, reduce the size of their governments and reduce onerous regulations to enhance the benefits of immigration. They can also make it easier for newcomers to work, start a business, transfer credentials and obtain occupational licenses, so that people move quickly from arrival to contribution.

Americans take pride in being a nation of immigrants and a beacon of hope and freedom for the world, with “The New Colossus” sonnet at the Statue of Liberty stating, “Give me your tired, your poor, your huddled masses yearning to breathe free.” And a record-high 79 percent of Americans tell Gallup that immigration is a good thing for our country.

For those who are concerned that immigration may drain our country of resources due to immigrants using more resources than they produce, our study provides some comfort. Not only does immigration enable people from around the world to take a chance on the American Dream; it enables Americans to benefit from the immigrants who take that chance.  

For many states, especially those still trying to grow, newcomers are not a cost to be absorbed. They are part of the next economy waiting to be built.

John Bitzan is the Menard Family Director of the Sheila and Robert Challey Institute for Global Innovation and Growth at North Dakota State University.

data.title: The hidden strategy behind Trump's latest Canada tariffs
data.description:
President Trump has reached deep into the trade law archives to wage his latest tariff campaign against Canada. 

On July 20, he invoked Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930 to impose a new 50 percent tariff on roughly $20 billion of Canadian imports. It is the first time the nearly century-old statute has been used this way. 

The products are a curious mix: hockey sticks, wine, cement, furniture, clothing, fishing rods and swimming pools. But the tariff itself isn’t the real story. The administration has quietly changed the legal and political theory of the U.S.-Canada trade war. 

For years, Washington treated Canada’s actions as retaliation. Trump is now calling them discrimination. 

Retaliation implies reciprocity. One country imposes tariffs; the other responds, and both claim they’re merely answering the other’s actions. Neither side looks innocent. The dispute becomes another round of escalation. Discrimination is different. Section 338 does not authorize tariffs simply because another country retaliates. It authorizes them when a country treats American commerce worse than commerce from other nations. 

The White House is no longer saying Canada merely answered American tariffs. It argues Canada deliberately disadvantaged U.S. producers while giving competitors from elsewhere better treatment. 

The administration points to Canadian auto quotas, provincial restrictions on American alcohol and dairy tariff-rate quotas that allegedly favor European cheese over U.S. exports. But those allegations are contestable, and one rests on shaky ground. The alcohol case turns on provincial liquor boards pulling U.S. bottles, a decision Ottawa did not make. Section 338 is the rare statute whose text lets Washington treat a province’s choice as the country’s discrimination. 

Instead of asking whether Canada’s response was justified, it is asking whether Canada crossed the line from reciprocal retaliation to unequal treatment. That is a much stronger negotiating position. 

For four decades, U.S.-Canada trade disputes followed the same script. Washington imposed duties. Ottawa retaliated. Canada challenged them under the North American Free Trade Agreement, the World Trade Organization or the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement. Lawyers won cases. Politicians settled. The cycle repeated. 

Trump’s Section 338 proclamation suggests the administration is losing patience with that cycle. Rather than waiting for another round of litigation, it is using an almost-forgotten statute to redefine the dispute itself. But the statute has limits: It caps the additional duties at 50 percent and ties them to the discrimination found. A flat rate across unrelated goods is hard to call the tailored offset the law contemplates. 

The choice of products reinforces that strategy. Many of the targeted industries are concentrated in Ontario and Quebec, Canada’s manufacturing heartland and its most politically influential provinces. The administration, meanwhile, largely spared the sectors America cannot easily replace, including energy, potash, critical minerals and products already covered by Section 232 tariffs. 

This is not indiscriminate protectionism. It is economic pressure designed to maximize political leverage while minimizing disruption to North American supply chains. 

The delayed implementation date tells the same story. The tariffs will not take effect until Aug. 19. That gives both governments weeks to negotiate over cars, alcohol and dairy before the duties bite. These tariffs are leverage first and revenue second. 

Prime Minister Mark Carney has already responded by promising intensified negotiations rather than another immediate round of retaliation. That may prove Trump’s biggest victory, but it need not be. Ottawa can negotiate and litigate at once. 

Canada will also argue that its measures were themselves responses to earlier U.S. tariffs, not independent acts of discrimination. Whether Section 338 applies in those circumstances has never been tested in modern courts.

Canada’s stronger ground is the remedy and the statute’s novelty. The law allows duties only to offset the specific discrimination found, and a flat 50 percent across unrelated goods offsets nothing in particular. Because Section 338 has never been used in its 96 years, no court has ever construed it — so reviving a dormant power this way, to override the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement, invites a challenge of its own. 

Canada will also argue that its measures were themselves responses to earlier U.S. tariffs — countermeasures, not discrimination. But Section 338 asks only that the president find discrimination, with no investigation and no process, so the finding itself is nearly unreviewable.

If the strategy succeeds, its implications extend far beyond Canada. Other governments that selectively target American exports in future trade disputes could find their actions recast not as lawful countermeasures but as discrimination subject to Section 338. 

The bigger warning is where this points next. The same discrimination theory is already aimed at Canada’s AI policy, privacy and data rules, the next measures Washington can recast as discrimination against American technology firms, even as it warns it would override signed trade deals. 

Trump has done more than impose another tariff. He has tried to rewrite the rules of North American trade diplomacy. 

Marc L. Busch is the Karl F. Landegger Professor of International Business Diplomacy at the Walsh School of Foreign Service, Georgetown University. Barry Appleton is Interim Director of the Balsillie Legal Advisory Centre at the Balsillie School of International Affairs and Co-Director of the Center for International Law at New York Law School.

data.title: Will John Healey fund the UK defense increases he previously demanded?
data.description:
Finance ministries tend to clash with defense ministries over levels of defense spending. In the U.S., the Office of Management and Budget generally is skeptical of Department of War proposals for major spending increases. The British Treasury is no different.

Under Keir Starmer, the Treasury frustrated the Ministry of Defence’s efforts to come closer to NATO’s spending targets of 3.5 per cent of Gross Domestic Product by 2035. 

Instead, defense spending was to rise to 2.7 percent of GDP by 2030. The Minister of Defence posited that it should reach 3 per cent by then. The minister resigned over what he considered to be serious budget shortfalls, arguing that “the Treasury [is] unwilling to commit the resources that the nation needs to defend the country at this time of rising threats.”

That Minister of Defense was John Healey. He is now Chancellor of the Exchequer in the new Andy Burnham government. He now is in charge of the Treasury that he had so bitterly criticized only weeks earlier. Burnham has said all the right things about increasing defense spending, except actually committing to do so. He has gone no further than to assert that “we’ll set out more on defense in due course.”

Wes Streeting, who took over the Ministry of Defence when Burnham moved into 10 Downing Street, has likewise insisted that the U.K. would honor its international commitments. He has not indicated how the government would find the £15 billion (through 2029) that Starmer added to the budget just before he left office without identifying a source. Nor has he identified the source for an additional £2.3 billion that the government’s Office for Budget Responsibility has estimated Britain must spend annually until 2030 to meet Healey’s 3 percent target.

Like Streeting, Healey reiterated Britain’s commitment to increasing defense spending, stating that “fiscal credibility is the bedrock for economic stability and for national security, and you heard the prime minister … say, in this more dangerous world, we will meet our commitments on defense to our international allies.” But he too gave no indication as to whether and how that commitment might actually be met.

One possible way that the government could have raised more money for defense would have been to issue defense bonds. At one point, Healey appeared to support the idea. That is no longer the case, however; the government has made it clear it will not issue bonds.

The Treasury is now indicating that it will raise £10.3 billion through budget transfers from other agencies. That may prove exceedingly difficult, given Burnham’s other spending commitments.

Burnham has promised to lift the country out of its economic doldrums; to improve public services; and at the same time, to consider raising individual tax exemptions, which have been frozen since 2021. The government has estimated that unfreezing the exemptions by 10 percent could cost the government more than £17 billion annually or roughly equal to the defense budget shortfall.

Not surprisingly, the Office for Budget Responsibility is warning that the U.K. is on an “unsustainable” trajectory. Under these circumstances, Healey faces a major challenge to find a way to support the defense increases that he so recently advocated.

Defense Minister Streeting has asserted that social justice and boosting Britain’s national security could “go hand in hand.” He has added that “it’s for the chancellor to make announcements on those matters but I certainly feel like we’ve got a firm friend in His Majesty’s Treasury.”

Burnham clarified on Wednesday that he had made “no immediate commitment” to raise the tax allowance, possibly indicating that defense spending might indeed be increased. Nevertheless, it is far from clear how firm a friend of defense spending Healey will prove to be. Even without the government’s increasing the tax exemption, Healey will confront multiple claimants for government funds and may simply adopt the Treasury’s traditionally cautious attitude toward defense spending. Where he sits may well prove to be where he stands.

In that regard, it is worth recalling that as Richard Nixon’s director of the Office of Management and Budget, Caspar Weinberger was a budget cutter, earning him the moniker “Cap the Knife.” Yet Weinberger became the Reagan Administration’s most powerful advocate for increases in the defense budget and oversaw the largest peacetime military buildup in American history.

Healey has moved in the opposite direction — from defense minister seeking an increase in his agency’s budget to leading the ministry that traditionally pushes for reductions in defense spending.

Whether in his new role he will continue to advocate for a defense budget that reaches 3 percent of GDP by 2030, or adopts the culture of his new ministry in light of Britain’s fiscal woes, very much remains an open question. 

Dov S. Zakheim is a senior adviser at the Center for Strategic and International Studies and vice chairman of the board for the Foreign Policy Research Institute. He was undersecretary of Defense (comptroller) and chief financial officer for the Department of Defense from 2001 to 2004 and a deputy undersecretary of Defense from 1985 to 1987.

data.title: Retirees may need $185K for healthcare, Fidelity estimates
data.description:
(NewsNation) — A 65-year-old retiring this year can expect to spend $185,500 on healthcare and medical expenses throughout their golden years.

That’s up 7.5 percent from Fidelity Investments’ estimate of $172,500 a year ago and more than double its inaugural estimate from 2002.

This year’s increase is nearly twice as large as the jump from 2024 to 2025 and reflects rising prices for care, increased utilization and growing costs tied to chronic conditions, according to Fidelity.

The latest estimate comes amid a major demographic transition, with roughly 4 million Americans turning 65 each year through 2027.

Fidelity’s $185,500 projection assumes retirees are enrolled in Medicare Parts A, B and D and includes premiums, copayments and other out-of-pocket costs for medical care and prescription drugs throughout retirement.

More than half of preretirees (54 percent) incorrectly believe Medicare will cover all of their health expenses, the financial institution found.

“Medicare is a critical part of retirement health coverage, but it does not eliminate every health care expense,” Steve Betts, head of Fidelity Health, said in a release.

Importantly, Fidelity’s estimate does not include long-term care costs, which can add up quickly for retirees who eventually need that care.

A recent analysis for the Health and Human Services Department estimated that
56 percent of people turning 65 between 2021 and 2025 will need some form of long-term care during their lifetime.

Consulting firm Milliman estimated earlier this year that 65-year-olds should set aside $135,000 to cover future paid long-term care costs.

Taken together, the estimates serve as a reminder that planning for retirement means preparing not only for everyday living expenses but also for significant medical and long-term care costs.

That financial pressure could also be keeping some Americans in the workforce longer than they intended.

Nearly 1 in 4 U.S. workers, about 23 million adults, say they’re staying in their current job even though they want to leave because they’re afraid of losing their health insurance, according to a new study from the West Health-Gallup Center on Healthcare in America.

data.title: Musk on his involvement in politics: 'I got carried away'
data.description:
Tech mogul Elon Musk reflected on his brief venture into politics in a new interview released Thursday, suggesting he “got carried away.”

“I think I got a little too involved in politics,” the Tesla and SpaceX CEO told The Economist. “I got carried away frankly.”

Musk took an increased interest in politics amid the 2024 elections, pouring more than $250 million into backing President Trump. After Trump won, he announced the billionaire would head up a cost-cutting effort known as the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE).

“The goal with the sort of DOGE stuff was to try to do something about the deficit, which is massive,” Musk said in the interview.

“We need to sort of take a close look at spending and make sure that money is being spent in sensible ways and that it is not wasted or spent on fraudulent activities,” he added. “And I got a lot of flak from this.”

DOGE was highly controversial, leading to sweeping job and funding cuts across the federal government. It officially shut down operations on July 4.

However, the initiative’s efforts had been waning for over a year following Musk’s departure in late May 2025.

The contentious nature of his work at DOGE spilled over onto his companies, particularly Tesla, which saw its stock sink sharply during his brief four-month stint in the Trump administration.

Musk became embroiled in a public dispute with Trump shortly after leaving the White House over the sweeping spending bill, known as the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, that was championed by the president.

The tech mogul threatened to launch a third party, but the effort never got off the ground. He and Trump appear to have mended their rift, with Musk attending a White House dinner last November and joining the president on a trip to China with other tech leaders in May.
